item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GAW would not have two of its members elected to the city council.

Nor would they get standard jobs in the city's department of infrastructure.

Nor — and this is important — would they devise a plan whose specific goal is the agonizingly horrible prolonged death of sign thieves. None of these signs are designed to be survivable; -5 is the least lethal, and it can (and will) make you starve yourself to death, assuming you don't die of thirst because the word "drink" isn't written on anything within your line of sight. If you have stolen one of these signs, the city council and department of infrastructure will murder you and your friends.

The whole point of GAW is that they're not murderous.

This SCP is wholly incompatible with the concept of GAW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm aware, but it does give off that vibe to me. It sounds like something GAW would do, approve of and then brag about. I mean, sorry if my wording felt a bit too forceful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is not tagged as a GAW item because it is not a GAW item, and if it were rewritten to be a GAW item, it would not be any good.

It is not, was not, and will not be GAW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this not tagged GAW?

Wait, it's not a GAW item?

HOW IS IT NOT A GAW ITEM?

I know rewrite is older, but still

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. It's a technical term for when your ability to swallow is fucked up, and little bits of food go into your lungs and then you get pneumonia. It's a common cause of death in Alzheimer's patients, for instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is "aspiration of food" the correct term?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … microgravity?

item.title: Re: New member with a suggestion
item.content:encoded: I am aware I am a bit late to the party but I just wanted to say that I find your suggestion favorable, but I am slightly biased as I like Cross-Linking as it allows users to follow a trail of SCPs, develop a story of sorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am surprised I am the first to suggest a cross test, but it seems to me that the Stop and Yield signs would be very useful in containing uncooperative SCiPs. One that comes to mind is SCP-1447, the angry monk tulpa. Toss SCP-1047-5 onto the wall when it is meditating, along with a hose piping in 'smoke', then have ███████ ████ give shutdown commands over a speaker. It at least had the potential to neutralize a Keter SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it does say that if the "room" isn't attached to anything (it uses the examples of tents and mobile homes) it can cause the whole structure to float, and that the restraining factor has typically been depressurization. Presumably if you sealed the structure, you could use it as a propulsion system with a force equal to negative gravity.

The catch is that you can't toggle it easily or throttle it down; you're always going to be producing -g worth of thrust in the direction it points while it's active. You'd want to deactivate it just about the time that you hit the lagrangian point, and then when you're landing you'd have to rapidly flip it between up and down to slow yourself without reversing your decent. Maybe if you mounted it on a robotic arm or something, and included some secondary thrusters… This is starting to sound like something from kerbal space program. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although it's a cool thought experiment and there are various infinite energy devices around (you'll likely love the "ectoentropic" tag) probably not. The big problem is that the effects only occur within the room it's placed, and don't extend past the walls. So an enclosed rocket would stay put, etc.

Damn sweet thought experiment, though. That's the sort of implication thinking that leads to awesome new tales. Keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, if you put the one way sign in a small box, fill the box with CO2 (or pot smoke, I guess?) and then attach the box to a turbine, have you just created free infinite energy? because it's going to move the box in the direction the arrow is pointing, which would move the turbine, which would change the arrow's direction, so it'd just keep spinning around.

Also, if you attach that box to a space ship, could you theoretically smoke your way to the moon? I mean sure, the Foundation has better ways of getting to the moon but STILL.

+1 for implications

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously, the town councillors got so fed up they made a pact with a dark being that took over their bodies via their teeth and used their souls to power each sign. Because you're a genius, Voct. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
six people who died at the same time and six types of sign.

… shit. I actually didn't realize that until just now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I love this. :D I enjoyed the sense you get through the article that these were made to punish street sign thieves, confirmed by the final addendum. I also particularly love

473 (four hundred and seventy-three) years of continuous operation.

and I want to note that this is a good use of the "numeral written out in parentheses" thing so many complain about, because it really drives home how long a period of time that is.

Also I just really want to know what's up with the teeth. The only connection I see is six people who died at the same time and six types of sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, nice touch with the effects.
My only complaint is a minor one, which is the wrong use of the word 'ironic' in the title. 'Literal' works better.

item.title: So another reason we need to reduce carbon emissions?
item.content:encoded: So another reason we need to reduce carbon emissions? :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So creepy and weird, original too. Things that seem quite hard to find nowadays. Upvoted.

item.title: New member with a suggestion
item.content:encoded:
Yo, I'm new and I basically joined to share this

This is kinda what I had in mind
This is kinda what I had in mind

The following is an addendum chronicling a failing initiative to utilize SCP-1047 in containment protocols. Having now read the guide on Cross-Linking, I see I am treading near some unfavorable territory, but I still believe this is worth putting forward:

Addendum: Due to their powerful, predictable effects, Dr. ██████ proposes the use of SCP-1047 in several containment procedures, the strength of their effects vastly outweighing the lack of focus and motivation their activation requirements affect on posted guards. Preliminary trials have met with mixed results:

Fixing multiple instances of SCP-1047-1 in a regular arrangement and focusing them on a central point have proven capable of creating semi-stable gravitational containment fields (minor instabilities arising from minute air pressure variance). When arranged spherically, they have proven cheap and effective alternatives to high-powered ELO-IID electromagnetic suspension. Propositions toward replacing systems at site███ to contain SCP-106 have met resistance, while implementation on SCP-271, SCP-555, SCP-608, andSCP-1449 are pending further review. SCP-334 not suggested due to the magnet's secondary containment effect on [REDACTED].

It was theorized that SCP-076-2 could be contained if SCP-1047-5 were placed within his chamber. If he were to lose motivation before [DATA EXPUNGED] Unfortunately, attempts to alter the atmosphere within SCP-076-1 proved entirely impossible.

Attempts at using any SCP-1047 against SCP-682 proved fruitless as it simply inhaled all the air from the room.

I figured that by making them all failed or pending proposals I wouldn't have to interject on some of the more popular pages that might be considered more "sacred". Also, it shows the Foundation to be competent in recognizing the usefulness of certain SCPs and their prudent utilization.

I don't know if I'm going by the right protocol, I just thought it'd be something nice to add to a page that has some potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The guys who made these signs should have taken a look at this.
Love the final line on this one. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, I'm satisfactorily disturbed. I upvoted this because teeth.

item.title: Pet peeve alert
item.content:encoded:
I have just changed "Cerenkov" to "Cherenkov" in the text, as the man in question was Russian and thus it is the latter that is the correct English transcription of his name.

According to an anecdote [citation needed] I have heard several times, the reason the form "Cerenkov" regularly shows up in English is that the paper which made Cherenkov known in the western world was published in Czechoslovakia. Czech transcription of the name in question is " Čerenkov" so I'm sure you can see how this has gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if multiple instances of 1047-1, pointing in different directions were placed in the same room and the activation conditions were met?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All six bodies had identical teeth.

Whaaat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Voct, this entire passage is absolutely amazing in its imagery.

I still think containment could be achieved at much less expense with outdoor containment with a field of telephone poles (for -1 to -5 of course)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It depends on how the anomaly was implemented: whether the anomaly affected the entire room all at once, or whether there were differences on a microsecond level. Did the effect spread out from the sign (and if so, how fast?) and stop when it reached the walls, or did it affect "the room as a whole entity", or…? What would happen to someone if only a part of their body was within the effect?

The highest resolution of the human sensory experience is… well, the highest-quality movies have a frame rate of considerably less than 100 frames per second, so we can reasonably conclude that for the purposes of ordinary human experience, "simultaneity" can be approximated as "happens in the same hundredth of a second". Lightspeed is about 1862 miles per HoaS. 55 miles per hour is ~ 9.7 inches per HoaS.

As the effect expands towards you, what you can see in its direction begins to slow down and become disjointed and disconnected as the light takes longer and longer to reach your eyes, producing an effect hideously similar to refraction. That's a good word, by the way - 'hideous'. It's very applicable in discussing the effects of 1047-6.

Within the room, time is hideously slowed and mass-energy is hideously inflated and… oh, there's lots more hideous consequences, some of which aren't at all obvious. Imagine that the effect has swallowed up your feet and ankles, for instance. The blood pumping within your feet has a mass-equivalent such that, right at the interface, the veins are being obliterated - but you don't feel it because the pain signal can't move fast enough, especially since the effect is spreading. If the effect were to stop halfway up your body, you'd have the impression of being jammed halfway through a forcefield — but it's not stopping, it's expanding until it hits the walls of the room. Organs fail, tissues disintegrate, cells rupture, even the more complex molecules tear themselves apart… if the effect were to spread out like a wave, pass through your body in a hundredth of a second, and then vanish instantly, you'd be dead. In less time than it would take for your own weight to collapse you from being a hideously human-shaped blob of sludge to just a hideous pile of sludge, you'd be converted into a hideous pile of sludge. Plus there's all the radiation being emitted from particles that would otherwise be superluminal…

Inside the room, though, the lightslow is still in effect. The stoners are being hideously irradiated and obliterated at a molecular level, they're definitely going to die hideously… but lightslow implies timeslow…

I think it's fair to say that the anomalous bureaucrats in the city administration didn't fully realize just what the hideous consequences of 1047-6 would be. Oh, they knew it would be a hideous, hideous death (which is what they wanted), but not like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted just for 1047-6. I read "lowers the speed of light to 55mph" and some small part of my mind exploded. This is so reality bending. I'm also more than a little curious to find out what is inside the room with the speed limit sign, and what the effect of living in that enviroment would do to a person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right, that wasn't very helpful and I apologise.

There's nothing really wrong with how it's written or formatted, I just think the central idea is incredibly flawed and over the top. I always feel that the best SCPs are the ones with a fairly subtle effect. Not only are the signs not subtle, but as I read through the article I was taken more and more out of the story by how deadly and, for want of a better word, anomalous they are. Also the weed trigger nearly made my eyes roll out of my head.

It's hard to really put my finger on, and I think that's probably what inspired my little snark above, but the whole thing is just… silly. I think a good writer can do a lot with a silly concept, but with how strict the SCP format is you don't have as much leeway.

I realise this isn't actually much better as far as criticism goes, but at least I used more words and was more polite :)

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded:
How would the Yield sign work, you ask?

Well, let's say you were watching TV, or had some music playing, or happen to be looking in the direction of something printed…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm. I see what you're saying, it might not be impressed with "Well, the mathematical average concentration of this area is below your threshold."

-edit-

I still think it would hurry things up because it'd be diffusing gas all around the boundary of the original room instead of just what was being exchanged by the two pumps, but no, not instant by any stretch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're entitled to your opinion and your downvote, Mericet, but could I ask you to expand on that?

"I just don't like it" would be one thing, but "this is the dumbest thing" is quite another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it'd work… the concentration on average would be lower, but there still is the local maximum in CO2 and weed smoke concentration delineated by the effect's boundary, and it's not going away soon.
Basically, you are sort of reasoning with a trigger as if it was an intelligence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the dumbest thing on the whole site :P

Edit: The SCP, not this comment >_>;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They don't have to wait 473 years for the 55 mph sign, all they have to do is saturate the areas surrounding the room in question with nitrogen and then demolish the walls of the room from the outside and it will either no longer be in a "room", ending the effect, or it will be in a much larger room with a low enough average concentration of CO2 and weed smoke particulates to deactivate the sign.

That aside, upvoted for overall greatness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't looked at this in a while

I like it a lot more now.

Voct's done it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because when they do manage to drain out enough atmosphere to deactivate the sign, they will have access to a device that can modify the speed of light within a given area.

And because as long as it's still in there causing this zone of fucked-up physics, it's dangerously radioactive.

Remember: as long as the Foundation doesn't actually have the sign in custody, it's not actually being secured or contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I love this revision. I do have one issue, though:

atmospheric concentrations are expected to drop below SCP-1047-6's activation parameters after 473 (four hundred and seventy-three) years of continuous operation

Why bother? Surely they don't honestly think that they can operate the device for that long. That's a titanic expenditure of resources for something that has absolutely no results for almost half a millennium.

Other than that, though, this is great. The mysterious similarity of the councilmen is a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rewrite is good, and my main problem with the original - its similarity to 1136 - is removed. However, the concept of street signs that kill you horribly if you steal them doesn't do much for me.

I am upvoting solely because of identical teeth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in the same boat. This is fucking great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck, Voct. I want to upvote this, but I already did the old one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These anomalous effects activate when an instance of SCP-1047 is taken from its standard pole,

So why doesn't the Foundation install some street poles at a Site and put the signs on the poles?

Autopsies revealed no anomalies, except that all six corpses had identical teeth.

Heheheh, I like the offhand strangeness of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice rewrite, Voct. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: Oops, posted twice. Sorry.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: "factor of $\sqrt{1-\frac{v_a v_b}{c^2}}$ "?

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: I think your formatting is off- should that be "Factor of sqrt(1-[vavb/c2])"?

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: That would be wonderful. We had a varient of that as a question in problems solving exam in 3rd year physics, the consequences are always at least a little amusing.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded:
Or… perhaps it's the speed of light in the room?

This'd be awesome. I mentioned it with respect to a failSCP, IIRC.
See.. imagine that was the case. Remember how velocity addition works when you have a set light speed, and how mass scales up?
Factor of sqrt(1 - v_a*v_b/c**2) .
Now imagine speed of light is 55mph.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded:
no, that's too abstract and not directly related to the meaning of the sign.

Hmmm.

I wonder what would happen if all the air molecules in the room could only move at 55 mph.

Or… perhaps it's the speed of light in the room?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, good work man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? I can't find it. =^.^=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhhh…anyone else notice that photo has a watermark?

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: Steal a 55 sign, turn 55 years old?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why am I getting a cultist vibe like the town council from Hot Fuzz?

I'm wondering if they have any funny street names that tourists like to steal signs from as well…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er. So, uh. May I rewrite this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
no, it's not Prometheus or the Factory, or MC&D or AWCY or anything like that. It was just some anomalous bureaucrats in the city administration who decided to set Ironic Deathtraps for those sign-stealing young punks, and committed mass suicide when the Foundation found out.

Imagine how frustrating this must be for the Foundation. They know who did it, and why it was done. But they don't have the slightest idea of how.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded:
I'm wondering how the Yield sign would work; anyone close enough to give you instructions would also be under the effect of the sign, so unable to give instructions, unless they shouted them through the wall/door?

This sounds like the sort of crazy Prometheus Labs, Inc. might produce, or possibly one of the other groups of interest? Need to read further into The Factory at some point.

Some really good ideas though, curious to see where this article goes.

item.title: Re: Aha!
item.content:encoded: Just fix the dialogue, which is currently rather too silly.
